content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN. Print a permutation of {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N} such that the greatest common divisor (GCD) of the numbers at odd indices is greater than the GCD of the numbers at even indices. If there are many such permutations, print the lexicographically largest permutation.

More formally, find the lexicographically largest permutation PPP such that GCD(P1,P3,P5,…)>GCD(P2,P4,P6,…)\text{GCD}(P_1, P_3, P_5, \ldots) > \text{GCD}(P_2, P_4, P_6, \ldots)GCD(P1​,P3​,P5​,…)>GCD(P2​,P4​,P6​,…), where PiP_iPi​ represents the number at index iii in the permutation.

If no solution exists, print -1.

**Note:** Permutation XXX is lexicographically larger than permutation YYY if Xi>YiX_i \gt Y_iXi​>Yi​, where iii is the first index where XXX and YYY differ.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The only line of each test case contains a single integer, NNN.


Output Format

Print the lexicographically largest permutation of {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N} that satisfies the given condition. Or, if no solution exists, print -1.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line. If no permutation satisfies the condition, print -1. Otherwise print N integers – the lexicographically largest permutation of {1,…,N} that satisfies GCD of numbers at odd indices > GCD of numbers at even indices. Numbers are separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: N=5 and N=17 are odd, so no permutation can satisfy the condition; output -1. N=6 is even, descending order 6 5 4 3 2 1 puts even numbers on odd indices (GCD=2) and odd numbers on even indices (GCD=1), thus condition holds and this permutation is lexicographically largest.
Both N values are even, so the descending permutation works.
N=1 is odd, impossible to satisfy the condition.

solution.approach: Observation: to have GCD of odd‑position elements larger than that of even‑position elements, all odd‑position elements must be even (so their GCD is at least 2) while at least one even‑position element must be odd (so its GCD can be 1). The count of odd positions is ceil(N/2) and the count of even numbers in 1..N is floor(N/2). Therefore a necessary condition is floor(N/2) >= ceil(N/2), which holds only when N is even. For even N we can simply place the numbers in descending order N, N-1, …, 1. In this order odd positions contain exactly the even numbers, whose GCD is 2, and even positions contain the odd numbers, whose GCD is 1, satisfying the requirement. Descending order is the lexicographically largest possible permutation, so it is the answer. If N is odd, the condition is impossible, output -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (to output the permutation).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the output.
